splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity trumps agency deference (Auer/Kisor deference) when interpreting ambiguous criminal sentencing provisions, such as the Sentencing Guidelines.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether courts should defer to the Sentencing Commission's anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions or instead apply the rule of lenity in favor of the defendant. This matters because it determines whether agencies can expand criminal liability through interpretive commentary rather than through formal rulemaking.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes priority over agency deference in criminal or quasi-criminal contexts, preventing agencies from using delegated interpretive power to expand criminal liability.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary takes priority over the rule of lenity, allowing anti-defendant interpretations of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether inchoate offenses such as conspiracy and attempt qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' under the career offender guideline (U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b)) when the guideline text does not mention them but Application Note 1 includes them.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' to include conspiracy and attempt, is entitled to controlling deference under Stinson/Kisor. This affects whether defendants with prior inchoate drug offenses qualify as career offenders with significantly enhanced sentences.

positions.label: Application Note 1 controls
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1's inclusion of inchoate offenses is an authoritative interpretation of the career offender guideline entitled to deference, so conspiracy and attempt qualify as controlled substance offenses.

positions.label: Guideline text excludes inchoate offenses
positions.summary: Holds that the unambiguous text of § 4B1.2(b) does not include inchoate offenses, and Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the guideline beyond its text, so it is not entitled to deference.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) to be disqualified from safety-valve relief, or whether meeting any single criterion suffices. This affects whether district courts must impose sentences without regard to statutory minimums for many drug offenders.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that 'and' in § 3553(f)(1) means defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three listed criminal-history criteria.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) may be considered as an 'extraordinary and compelling reason' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A), and whether the Sentencing Commission's policy statement (U.S.S.G. § 1B1.13(b)(6)) permitting such consideration exceeds its statutory authority.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in law as a basis for compassionate release. Some circuits hold that such changes can never contribute to extraordinary and compelling reasons, while others permit courts to consider them, especially after the Sentencing Commission's 2023 amendment to § 1B1.13(b)(6). This affects thousands of prisoners serving sentences under now-outdated mandatory minimums.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot support compassionate release
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot constitute an extraordinary and compelling reason for sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A), and the Sentencing Commission's contrary policy statement exceeds its authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that non-retroactive changes in law may be considered, at least as one factor among others, in evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons warrant a sentence reduction under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Esteras (Moore, J., dissenting)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly punish defendants for violations of supervised release when revoking supervision and imposing a new term of imprisonment, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to sanctions for the breach of trust.
splits.description: The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for their underlying conduct when revoking supervised release, while other circuits limit the purpose of revocation sentences to sanctioning the breach of the court's trust. This split affects the length and justification of sentences imposed upon revocation of supervised release.

positions.label: Courts may punish for underlying conduct
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may expressly punish defendants for the conduct underlying a supervised release violation, placing the Sixth Circuit at the extreme of the split.

positions.label: Revocation sentences limited to breach of trust
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences should be based on the breach of the court's trust rather than punishment for the new criminal conduct, consistent with the policy statement framework.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Esteras (Griffin, J., dissenting, citing other circuits)

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines (U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b)) when the state schedule of controlled substances is broader than the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance underlying a state conviction to also be controlled under federal law. The Eighth Circuit holds there is no federal-law limitation, while other circuits require the substance to be federally controlled. This significantly affects career offender and other Guidelines enhancements.

positions.label: No federal-law limitation
positions.summary: Holds that § 4B1.2(b)'s reference to 'an offense under state law' encompasses any substance controlled under state law, with no requirement that it also be controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a controlled substance offense only if the substance at issue is also controlled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal deadline for a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the civil 30-day deadline or the criminal 14-day deadline under the Federal Rules of Appellate Procedure.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a motion for attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment, filed within a criminal case, is treated as a civil matter (30-day appeal deadline) or a criminal matter (14-day appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined what it described as the majority view that the civil deadline applies because such a motion is civil in nature despite arising from a criminal case.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies (30 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, so the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies (14 days)
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case and governed by the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b).

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: United States v. Manchester Farming Partnership

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether appreciation in property value that occurs between the initial Chapter 13 filing and conversion to Chapter 7 belongs to the bankruptcy estate or the debtor. The Ninth Circuit holds it belongs to the estate based on the broad scope of § 541(a), while other circuits hold it belongs to the debtor based on the structure and policy of the Bankruptcy Code.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad scope of 'property of the estate' under § 541(a), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor based on the Bankruptcy Code's structure, policies, and legislative history of § 348(f).

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Castleman dissent citing contrary circuit authority

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as 'effectively unreviewable' on appeal from the final judgment.
splits.description: The D.C. Circuit held that maintaining medical privacy is a value of sufficiently 'high order' to warrant immediate appellate review of sealing denials under the collateral order doctrine. Other circuits have not uniformly extended the collateral order doctrine to sealing disputes involving medical records, creating potential divergence on the boundaries of immediate appealability.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal medical records is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of high order that would be irreparably lost without immediate review.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that sealing orders generally do not satisfy the collateral order doctrine's requirements and must await final judgment for appellate review.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Referenced by D.C. Circuit as recognizing strong medical privacy interests but applying different framework
